title: Graphs I: Traversal & Union-Find
summary: This is the single most-tested OA topic for you: Microsoft loves graphs+DP, Flipkart loves DFS. You have never done graphs before, but the good news is that ~90% of graph problems reduce to five templates — BFS, DFS, the grid idiom, topological sort, and Union-Find. Get those into muscle memory and a whole class of "hard" problems becomes mechanical.

notes.heading: Recognizing a graph problem (the word "graph" never appears)
notes.body:
Here is the dirty secret of graph questions in OAs: they almost never say "graph". They hand you a grid, a list of `edges`, `prerequisites`, or `connections`, or say "n servers and the cables between them" — and expect *you* to say "this is a graph". Naming the model is half the battle, and interviewers explicitly grade it.

Train yourself on these triggers:

| You see… | You think… |
|---|---|
| a 2-D grid of cells | implicit graph, neighbors = 4 adjacent cells |
| "can you get from A to B?", "connected", "groups", "islands" | reachability / connected components → **DFS** (or DSU) |
| "minimum moves", "fewest steps", "nearest", on **unweighted** edges | shortest path → **BFS** (level = distance) |
| "finish B before A", "build order", dependencies | **topological sort** (and cycle detection for free) |
| edges arriving one by one + "are these two in the same group yet?" | **Union-Find (DSU)** |

> [!KEY]
> BFS vs DFS is not a style choice. If the question asks for a **shortest number of steps** on an unweighted graph, it MUST be BFS — DFS finds *a* path, not the shortest one. If you only need reachability or components, either works; DFS is usually less code.

> [!EXAM]
> In a timed OA, the phrase "minimum number of operations/steps/minutes" on any state you can transform is a flashing BFS sign — even when there is no visible grid. Word Ladder is exactly this: words are nodes, one-letter changes are edges.

notes.heading: The two engines: adjacency list + DFS/BFS
Grids are graphs in disguise
Topological sort: ordering under dependencies
Union-Find (DSU): components as edges stream in
Pitfalls, edge cases, and how to narrate
notes.body:
The bugs below are, in my experience, where graph OA points actually die. Read this list before every timed session until it is boring:

- **Forgetting `visited` / marking too late.** In BFS, mark when you **push**, not when you pop (the `dist[v] == -1` check does this cleanly). Otherwise: exponential enqueues, TLE.
- **Bounds check order.** Range-check `nr, nc` *before* indexing `grid[nr][nc]`.
- **Directed vs undirected.** Add both `adj[u].push_back(v)` and `adj[v].push_back(u)` only for undirected graphs. Prerequisites are directed — add one direction, and be deliberate about which way it points (`b → a` = "b before a").
- **Boolean visited on a directed graph** detects revisits, not cycles. Use Kahn or 3-color DFS.
- **Recursion depth.** Grid DFS on ~10⁶ cells can blow the call stack. If bounds are large, switch to the iterative BFS template.
- **Off-by-one in level counting.** For "minimum steps/minutes", increment the counter once per **level** (snapshot `q.size()` before the inner loop), not once per node.

> [!EXAM] Edge cases to say out loud (and test)
> Empty grid / zero nodes; a single node; a **disconnected** graph (your outer for-loop over all nodes must exist); self-loops and duplicate edges; already-fully-connected input; unreachable targets (return -1 / leave `dist == -1`).

**Interview narration script** — do this every single time, it is graded:

1. "This is a graph — nodes are ___, edges mean ___." Name the model explicitly.
2. "I need shortest steps on an unweighted graph, so **BFS**" — or — "I just need reachability/components, so **DFS**." One sentence of justification.
3. State complexity up front: almost everything here is $O(V + E)$ time and space; on a grid $V = R \cdot C$ and $E \approx 4RC$, so $O(R \cdot C)$.
4. Build the adjacency list out loud, then write the memorized template. Mention `visited` *before* writing the loop — it signals you won't infinite-loop.
5. If dependencies/ordering appear: "topological sort — I'll use Kahn's indegree method, which also detects cycles for free." That sentence alone lands the whole problem class.

> [!KEY]
> Speed here is not cleverness, it is **typing templates you already know**. Your goal for this module: write grid-BFS, Kahn, and the DSU struct from memory, each in under three minutes.

problems.name: Flood Fill
problems.note: The simplest grid DFS/BFS — use it to burn in the direction-array idiom.

problems.name: Number of Islands
problems.note: Count connected components in a grid — the canonical grid-DFS; each fresh start is one island.

problems.name: Max Area of Island
problems.note: Grid DFS that *returns a size* — accumulate cell counts as you traverse.

problems.name: Rotting Oranges
problems.note: Multi-source BFS: push all rotten oranges at level 0; levels = minutes elapsed.

problems.name: 01 Matrix
problems.note: Multi-source BFS from **all** 0-cells at once — nearest-source distance for every cell.

problems.name: Walls and Gates
problems.note: **Premium** — multi-source BFS from every gate; if locked, 01 Matrix drills the identical idea.

problems.name: Clone Graph
problems.note: DFS/BFS while building a hashmap old→new node; the map doubles as your visited set.

problems.name: Course Schedule
problems.note: Cycle detection in a directed graph = topological-sort feasibility (Kahn: did you take all n?).

problems.name: Course Schedule II
problems.note: Return an actual topological order — Kahn's popped sequence IS the answer.

problems.name: Pacific Atlantic Water Flow
problems.note: Reverse the flow: BFS/DFS *inward* from both ocean borders (uphill only), intersect the two reach-sets.

problems.name: Surrounded Regions
problems.note: Mark border-connected regions first, then flip everything else — solve the complement.

problems.name: Number of Connected Components in an Undirected Graph
problems.note: **Premium** — the textbook DSU problem (components counter); if locked, drill Graph Valid Tree instead.

problems.name: Graph Valid Tree
problems.note: **Premium** — DSU checklist: exactly n−1 edges, no cycle, one component.

problems.name: Redundant Connection
problems.note: DSU: the first edge whose `unite` returns false connects already-joined nodes — that's the answer.

problems.name: Word Ladder
problems.note: Shortest transformation = BFS on an *implicit* graph where words are nodes and one-letter edits are edges.

deepdives.q: **Rotting Oranges** — a grid holds rotten (2), fresh (1), and empty (0) cells. Each minute every rotten orange rots its 4-adjacent fresh neighbors. Return the number of minutes until no fresh orange remains, or -1 if that never happens.

**Course Schedule** — given `numCourses` and `prerequisites[i] = [a, b]` meaning course `b` must be taken before course `a`, decide whether all courses can be finished. (Course Schedule II asks for an actual valid order.)

**Pacific Atlantic Water Flow** — water on a cell flows to 4-adjacent neighbors of equal or lower height. The Pacific touches the top and left borders, the Atlantic the bottom and right. Return every cell from which water can reach **both** oceans.

**Redundant Connection** — a tree with n nodes needs n−1 edges, but you're given n edges (exactly one extra, forming exactly one cycle). Return the edge that can be removed to restore a tree; if several qualify, return the one that appears last in the input.
deepdives.answer:
**Why DSU nails it.** Process the edges in input order and `unite` their endpoints. A tree grows by always joining two *previously disconnected* components. The moment an edge tries to connect two nodes **already in the same component**, that edge closes the cycle — it is the redundant one.

[[fig:m10-dsu-merge]]

And the tie-breaking comes free: since exactly one cycle exists, only its edges "qualify", and scanning in input order means the first `unite` failure you hit is the *last-in-input-order* edge of that cycle — exactly what the problem asks for.

```cpp
vector<int> findRedundantConnection(vector<vector<int>>& edges) {
    int n = edges.size();
    DSU dsu(n + 1);                               // nodes are 1-indexed
    for (auto& e : edges)
        if (!dsu.unite(e[0], e[1]))               // unite returns false => same set
            return e;                             // this edge made the cycle
    return {};
}
```

That's it — the DSU struct from the notes plus a single `if`. Which is the real lesson: **once DSU is in your fingers, a chunk of "hard" graph problems collapses to a few lines.**

> [!PITFALL]
> Nodes here are numbered `1..n`, so construct `DSU(n + 1)` — sizing it `n` is an off-by-one crash. And keep path compression + union by rank in the struct; without them each `find` can degrade to $O(n)$ and TLE.

**Complexity:** n `unite` calls at inverse-Ackermann cost each → **time $O(n \cdot \alpha(n)) \approx O(n)$, space $O(n)$** ($\alpha$ is effectively a small constant).

**What generalizes:** Graph Valid Tree is the same check plus "exactly n−1 edges and one component"; Accounts Merge, Kruskal's MST (Module 11), and any "does this edge add new connectivity?" question all ride on the same `unite`-returns-false signal.

quiz.q: An OA asks: "given an unweighted maze grid, find the **minimum number of moves** from the entrance to the exit." Which tool, and why?
options: DFS — it explores every possible path, so it will find the minimum
BFS — it explores in level order, so the first time the exit is reached is via a fewest-moves path
Union-Find — cells that connect belong to the same set
Topological sort — each move depends on the previous one
quiz.explanation: "Minimum steps on an unweighted graph" is the BFS signature: the queue processes all cells at distance d before any at distance d+1, so first arrival = shortest distance. DFS (option A) finds *a* path but can wander arbitrarily far before finding the short one — you'd need to exhaust every path to be sure, which is exponential. DSU answers connectivity, not distance; topo sort needs a DAG of dependencies, which a maze isn't.

quiz.q: In the BFS template, a node is marked visited (`dist[v]` set) **when it is pushed**. A student instead marks nodes when they are **popped**. What happens?
options: Nothing — the two versions are equivalent
It only breaks on directed graphs
The same node can be enqueued many times via different neighbors before its first pop — the queue blows up and the program TLEs
BFS returns wrong distances even on a simple path graph
quiz.explanation: Between a node's first push and its first pop, every other neighbor that discovers it sees it as 'unvisited' and enqueues it again — on dense graphs this multiplies the queue enormously (potentially exponentially in layered graphs). Distances on a path graph would still come out right (option D is false), which is what makes this bug sneaky: it's a *performance* corruption that only shows as TLE on big tests.

quiz.q: You run Kahn's algorithm on a directed graph with `n = 9` nodes and the final `order.size()` is 7. What do you know for certain?
options: The graph contains at least one cycle — the 2 missing nodes never reached indegree 0
The graph is disconnected
Exactly two nodes had indegree 0 at the start
There is a bug — Kahn's algorithm always outputs all n nodes
quiz.explanation: A node is output only when its indegree drops to 0, i.e. all its prerequisites were output first. Nodes on (or exclusively fed by) a cycle wait on each other forever and never emerge — so a short output is a *proof* of a cycle. Disconnection (option B) is irrelevant: an isolated node starts at indegree 0 and is output immediately, so disconnected DAGs still produce all n nodes.

quiz.q: In the DSU template, `unite(a, b)` returns `false` exactly when…
options: a or b is out of range
the two roots have equal rank
union by rank had to swap a and b
`find(a) == find(b)` — the nodes are already in the same component, which is how Redundant Connection detects its cycle edge
quiz.explanation: `unite` first compresses both arguments to their roots; if the roots coincide, the nodes are already connected by some path, so adding this edge would close a cycle — it returns false and changes nothing. Equal ranks (option B) and the swap (option C) are normal parts of a *successful* union. This one boolean is the entire algorithmic content of Redundant Connection and Graph Valid Tree.

quiz.q: Rotting Oranges on the grid `[[2,1,1],[0,1,0]]` (row 0: rotten, fresh, fresh; row 1: empty, fresh, empty). Using correct per-level counting, how many minutes until nothing is fresh?
options: -1 (some orange is unreachable)
quiz.explanation: Minute 1: the source (0,0) rots its only fresh neighbor (0,1). Minute 2: (0,1) rots both (0,2) and (1,1) in the same level. All fresh oranges are gone after 2 minutes. If you incremented the counter per *node* instead of per level you'd report 3 or more — the classic off-by-level bug. -1 is wrong because every fresh orange is 4-connected to the source.

quiz.q: What is the time complexity of BFS over an entire R×C grid (the whole flood-fill / islands family)?
options: $O(R \cdot C \cdot (R + C))$
$O((R \cdot C)^2)$
$O(R \cdot C)$ — each cell enters the queue at most once and has at most 4 edges
quiz.explanation: The grid is a graph with V = R·C nodes and E ≈ 4·R·C edges, and BFS/DFS are O(V + E) because the visited mark guarantees each node is processed once and each edge looked at a constant number of times. That collapses to O(R·C). Option B is what you get if you *forget* visited or launch a fresh full search per cell — which is exactly why naive Pacific-Atlantic TLEs.

quiz.q: Why can't a plain boolean `visited` array detect a cycle in a **directed** graph via DFS?
options: Because revisiting an already-finished node is perfectly normal in a DAG (a cross edge) — you need a third state, "in the current recursion stack", to distinguish a true back-edge
Because boolean arrays can't store enough information for graphs with more than 2^31 nodes
It can, as long as you reset the array between the outer-loop DFS calls
Because directed graphs must be stored as adjacency matrices, not lists
quiz.explanation: In a directed acyclic graph, two different paths can legally converge on the same node — hitting a visited node proves nothing about cycles. A cycle exists only if DFS meets a node that is still *on the current recursion stack* (a back-edge), hence the 3-color scheme: unvisited / in-stack / done. Option C makes things worse (re-exploration, possible exponential blowup). Kahn's algorithm avoids the whole issue by counting output nodes.

m10-bfs-levels.caption: BFS from source **S** expands in rings: every node in a column is first reached at the same level, and that level **is** its shortest distance. This is why "minimum steps, unweighted" always means BFS.

m10-grid-dirs.caption: The direction-array idiom: from cell **(r, c)**, index `k = 0..3` into `dr`/`dc` generates the up, down, left, right neighbors. Always bounds-check `nr, nc` **before** indexing the grid.

m10-multi-source.caption: Multi-source BFS: both rotten cells (marked **0**) enter the queue together at level 0. Each cell's number is the minute it rots — its distance to the **nearest** source. The two waves race and meet in the middle.

m10-kahn-peel.caption: Kahn's algorithm on a dependency DAG. Nodes **0** and **1** start at indegree 0 (no unmet prerequisites) and seed the queue. Taking them decrements their neighbors' indegrees, releasing 2 and 3 next. If fewer than n nodes ever come out, the leftovers form a cycle.

1</text><text x="20" y="278" class="t-sm t-muted">take 0 and 1 → indegrees drop → 2 then 3 join the queue; output shorter than n = cycle</text></svg>

m10-dsu-merge.caption: DSU as a cycle detector: nodes 2 and 3 already share root **1**, so when edge (2, 3) arrives, `unite` finds equal roots and returns false — that edge is the redundant one. Node 5's set has a different root, so uniting across sets would succeed.
m10-dsu-merge.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 660 260" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Two union-find trees; a dashed edge between two nodes with the same root closes a cycle"><text x="20" y="24" class="t-bold">unite(2, 3) fails → edge (2, 3) closes the cycle</text><line x1="99" y1="176" x2="139" y2="117" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/><line x1="201" y1="176" x2="161" y2="117" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/><line x1="340" y1="173" x2="340" y2="121" class="s-line" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow)"/><line x1="107" y1="190" x2="193" y2="190" class="s-bad" stroke-width="2" stroke-dasharray="6 4"/><circle cx="150" cy="100" r="17" class="f-accent-soft s-accent"/><text x="150" y="105" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono t-accent">1</text><circle cx="90" cy="190" r="17" class="f-panel s-line"/><text x="90" y="195" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono">2</text><circle cx="210" cy="190" r="17" class="f-panel s-line"/><text x="210" y="195" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono">3</text><circle cx="340" cy="100" r="17" class="f-panel s-line"/><text x="340" y="105" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono">4</text><circle cx="340" cy="190" r="17" class="f-panel s-line"/><text x="340" y="195" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono">5</text><text x="150" y="66" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-accent">root</text><text x="150" y="232" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-bad">new edge (2,3): same root → cycle!</text><text x="340" y="232" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">different set: unite would succeed</text><text x="430" y="95" class="t-mono t-sm">find(2) = 1</text><text x="430" y="121" class="t-mono t-sm">find(3) = 1</text><text x="430" y="147" class="t-sm">same root → unite returns false</text><text x="430" y="173" class="t-sm t-muted">path compression flattens each walk</text></svg>
